title: Fantasy Dungeon Quest Hooks
description: The reasons people go into dark underground places — not just treasure, but the range of motivations that send people toward danger: rescue, research, obligation, history, and the specific pulls that only a dungeon can create.

entries.result: A scholar has been missing for two weeks. She went to document ruins beneath the old citadel that have been 'sealed for study' for seventy years. The university is asking quietly.
A local well has started producing water that smells of iron and sulfur. The well shaft connects to a cistern that connects to something lower.
A thieves' guild held a vault in the undercroft of a building that collapsed. The guild is not in a position to recover it openly. They're hiring.
A child was taken into a cave system by something that left tracks but no blood. The tracks go further down than anyone has been.
The party holds a map to a location that turns out to be accessible only from beneath — the entrance is underground, and the map only covers the approach.
A cult has been using an old dungeon as a meeting place. They've been there long enough to have improved the facilities, which means they've also found what was there before them.
A dwarf clan asserts rights to a delve abandoned by their ancestors three generations ago. They need someone to go in first and establish that the rights are worth asserting.
The ruins beneath a city include a section of what appears to be a significantly older settlement. An academic society is funding expeditions. The previous two expeditions didn't come back.
A prisoner was moved to a secure location described only as 'the old holding cells.' The location is underground and the guards who moved the prisoner haven't reported back.
An alchemist needs a sample of a material that occurs only in a specific geological formation, deep, in a cave system known for its occupants.
The party owes a debt to someone who wants something retrieved from a place they describe with precision and access to which they conspicuously don't have.

A mine expansion punched through a wall into something that was sealed. The miners who went in to investigate have been giving vague reports and the mining company wants someone not employed by them to take a look.

A magical catastrophe from a century ago left an underground complex in a condition that is now stabilizing. The residue from that event is either dangerous waste or rare ingredient, depending on who you ask.
The site of a famous siege has an underground component that history books don't address. A historian believes the siege's outcome depended on that component and wants evidence.
A temple's relics were moved to a 'safe location' during a civil war and were never moved back. The current temple leadership is embarrassed that the previous leadership apparently forgot where.
A golem is still down there, still following its last instructions, and those instructions involve a task that has consequences at the surface.
Someone is selling maps to a dungeon that contains real treasure. The maps are accurate. The person selling them is the one who created the dungeon and placed the treasure, and they want it found.

An old dragon's lair has been vacant for sixty years. Something has moved in. Whatever moved in is not a dragon, and the neighboring communities would prefer to know what it is before planning accordingly.
A guild representative arrives with a contract: recover a specific item from a specific room in a specific dungeon, delivery in five days. The rate is generous. The timeline is why.
A dungeon has been used as a prison for a being that has been imprisoned for a very long time. The binding is failing. The question is whether to reinforce it, negotiate with it, or help it leave.
A wizard left their library in a pocket dimension accessible only from a specific location deep in an old complex. The wizard is dead. The library is not, exactly, and someone needs to address it.
The local crime lord wants to move an operation to a specific underground location. They've sent four groups to clear it. None returned. They're prepared to be embarrassed and hire professionals.

A map from two hundred years ago shows a tunnel that would allow passage through a mountain range now controlled by a hostile power. The map is accurate. Whether the tunnel is passable is a different question.

A series of tremors has opened a section of underground that has been sealed since the kingdom's founding. The founding documents indicate what's down there. The founding documents were written to discourage investigation.
A young noble took a dare and went into a notorious local dungeon with two friends. The dare was three days ago. The noble's family is willing to pay for a recovery, retrieval, or accounting.
An architect is designing a building over a site that her survey shows is hollow beneath. She needs someone to determine what the hollow is before the foundation is laid.
A creature that was domesticated by a previous expedition has now wandered into a dungeon and its handlers want it back before it or something down there gets hurt.
The dungeon beneath the castle is the castle's oldest part. The current owners know very little about it. Something from it has started appearing in the upper stories at night.
A secret order maintains a reliquary in an underground site and has lost contact with the keeper. They suspect something has changed but don't know what.
A city is planning to expand its sewers. The expansion will pass through a section of ancient construction. The city wants someone to assess what they'll be going through before workers do.
A weapon that was supposed to be destroyed was hidden instead. The person who hid it is dead. The description of its location is an underground complex from a hundred years ago.
The dungeon is occupied by a community of creatures who have been there for generations and who have sent a representative to the surface to negotiate something.
A plague is being traced to its source. The current leading theory is an underground water source that connects to a specific complex of caverns with a specific kind of resident.
A legendary treasure exists, and its location is a dungeon that is well-documented, well-known, and has been entered dozens of times without anyone reaching the treasure. Someone has figured out why.
An inheritance dispute centers on a document that was stored in a family vault that is now inside an abandoned and subsequently occupied complex.
A sinkhole opened beneath a residential street. The investigation determined the street was built over an underground structure whose occupants are actively angry about the intrusion.
A temple constructed over a pre-existing site has been getting requests — from below — for services it doesn't offer. The priests want to know what they're living on top of.
Something in a dungeon is broadcasting a regular magical signal. Scholars have decoded it as a distress call. It's been going for an unknown length of time. It may still be appropriate to answer.

A dungeon was the site of a battle between two magical factions. One side won. The winner's work is still active and has been maintaining the loser's sealed containment for a century. The maintenance is failing.

An explorer compiled a complete guide to a dungeon and published it. The publisher wants a follow-up covering the sections the original author marked 'to be addressed in subsequent volume,' which was never written.
A drug is being produced using materials that can only come from a specific cave system. The source needs to be found, assessed, and either secured or eliminated.
A retired adventurer spent forty years exploring a specific dungeon and never reached its deepest level. They've written everything down and they want someone to finish it for them before they die.
A bandit group has used a dungeon as a base for two years. The local authority wants it cleared. The local authority is also apparently why the bandits are there, a detail that emerges mid-operation.

A door was sealed by an archmage who is no longer available to unseal it. Something behind it was sealed with the door. The current magical academy believes the contents are important enough to justify the risk of opening.
The dungeon was a research facility. The research was discontinued. The experiment was not.

A trade guild has discovered that a competitor has been using underground passages to move goods past the city's customs checkpoints. They want the passages mapped and want to know if they can use them too.
A necromancer's tower collapsed thirty years ago. The necromancer's work didn't. The underground part of the complex is intact and has been active ever since.

A prophecy describes the party reaching 'the room below the room that has no floor' and finding something there. The location has been identified. The prophecy doesn't clarify whether what they find is good.
An expedition left marking stones at regular intervals. The marking stones lead to a point deep underground and then stop. Someone needs to continue past that point and find out why they stopped.

A city below a city has been discovered. It predates the current city by five centuries. The historical society wants scholars to document it. The scholars want guards. The guards want to know what happened to the people who built it.
A dungeon is being considered for conversion into a tourist attraction by an entrepreneurial noble. The noble wants an assessment of which parts are safe, which can be made safe, and which cannot.

A dragon wants something from its old lair and is willing to compensate handsomely whoever retrieves it, the lair now being occupied by things the dragon finds beneath its dignity to personally address.

The party's employer hasn't specified what they're looking for, only what it looks like, where it is, and what they're prepared to pay. The reasonable inference is that the employer knows the dungeon better than they're admitting.
A siege tunnel from an ancient war connects two cities that are now enemies again. Someone has discovered this and is making preparations. Someone else needs to get there first.
A pilgrim route passes through an underground section that has been closed for safety reasons. The pilgrims go anyway. Something has started happening to the ones who complete the route.
An old complex contains a magical library. A faction that should not have access to it has found the entrance. The party has been asked to arrive before them.
A dwarf fortress was abandoned in suspicious circumstances. The descendants want to know what happened. A city was built over the entrance two hundred years ago, and access is complicated.
The dungeon's occupants have sent an ambassador to negotiate terms. The party is asked to attend the meeting as neutral witnesses.
An engineer has determined that a dungeon section underlies a building that will collapse if the dungeon section is disturbed. Something in the dungeon section needs to be disturbed.

A criminal organization stole something from a noble family and hid it in a dungeon. The noble family hired the party. The criminal organization also hired the party, not knowing. The dungeon belongs to a third party.
A magical disaster created a pocket of preserved time in a dungeon. Inside, things are as they were a century ago. The party needs something from that time.
A ghost is haunting the surface, connected to a location underground where they died. The ghost is dangerous and wants something the party can only understand by going where they died.

Centuries of adventurer traffic has left an ecological impact on a dungeon. A scholar wants to study it. The study requires samples from inhabited areas. The inhabitants are not part of the study but are relevant to its logistics.
A dungeon has a resident who has been there for decades and who is not hostile, is not treasure, and who has sent word they'd like to discuss something with people from the surface.

Someone is systematically looting dungeons in the region, taking specific items and leaving the rest. The party has been hired to find out who, with the understanding that the answer will be surprising.
A tunnel needs to be dug to rescue someone. The tunnel needs to pass through a dungeon section. The dungeon was not part of the rescue plan.
An ancient text describes a specific room in a specific complex where a specific ritual can be performed. The ritual's purpose is disputed. The room's existence is not.

The dungeon is not dangerous — it's been cleared dozens of times and is considered safe. Something has moved in recently that has made it not safe, and a farming community nearby needs the situation resolved before planting season.
A child's imaginary friend has been leading them to a specific entrance for months. The child is nine. The imaginary friend has specific geographic knowledge that a child shouldn't have.
A vault was never successfully opened. The combination is believed to be in a location inside the dungeon. The vault's current owner is willing to share what's inside in exchange for the combination.
An underground river has been redirected by something and is now flooding a different area. The redirection appears intentional. Someone or something below changed the course deliberately.
A contract was signed in a dungeon chamber and witnessed by its resident. The contract is being disputed. The witness needs to be consulted.
A dungeon was the site of a mass death. The dead are not at rest. The specific nature of the unrest suggests that the circumstances of the death were not what the official record says.
A merchant consortium has been paying for regular supply runs to something underground. The merchant who handled the runs has died, and no one else knows what they were supplying or to whom.
A dungeon has a reputation for driving explorers mad. A physician believes the cause is environmental rather than supernatural and wants samples that require being in the affected area to collect.
A holy symbol was lost underground. Its recovery is a matter of religious obligation. The religion in question doesn't have the personnel for this kind of operation.

A dungeon was described in a famous novel as fictional. An explorer has found it and documented that it's real and accurate in detail. The author has been dead for forty years. Someone wrote that book with insider knowledge.
Something underground is disrupting magical work on the surface. The effect is specific enough to identify the location. The cause needs to be identified before it can be addressed.

A warlord is planning to use an old underground complex as a staging area. The complex's current occupants don't know yet. A third party needs someone to ensure this plan fails, and they're not being clear about why they care.
An old complex contains the tools and materials of a master craftsperson. The craft is lost. The tools still work. Someone with the skill to use them would be significantly interested in this news.
A dungeon is accessed through a portal that only opens once per year. The last group that went in hasn't come out. The portal opens in two days.
A city's founding myth says a specific object was placed underground during the founding ceremony. Archaeological evidence supports the myth. Recovering the object would have political implications.

A bounty has been placed on something that lives underground. The bounty has been outstanding for fifteen years, which says something about the difficulty. The bounty is now worth enough to attract the party.
A dungeon's highest room is a skylight. Climbing up through the dungeon would take the party somewhere otherwise inaccessible from outside, which is why someone is offering to pay for it.
A seal placed on a dungeon by a long-dead order is about to expire. The order placed the seal for good reasons. The party has three weeks to determine what those reasons were.

An underground complex connects two regions that are separated on the surface by impassable terrain. Both sides know it exists. Neither side controls it. The party has been hired by one side not to mention the other side.
Something is eating the support structures of a dungeon, destabilizing it progressively. If it reaches a specific chamber, the consequence at the surface will be significant.
A survey of a dungeon produced a map that shows a room that later expeditions have been unable to find. The original surveyors are dead. Their notes say the room is important.
A dungeon has been generating magical energy for an unknown purpose. The accumulation has been noticed by multiple parties. Each party has a different idea about what should be done with it.

A person who died underground left a will specifying that their possessions be retrieved and distributed. The possessions are in the room where they died. The room is in a part of the dungeon they were the last person to explore.
A political opponent of the party's employer was last seen entering a dungeon complex. The employer wants to know if they're still there, and in what condition.
The party's healer needs a specific rare component for a cure. It grows only underground, in a specific environment, which is occupied.

A dungeon contains a room that acts as a scrying focus — someone inside the room can observe anyone on the surface who has been inside the room. It's been in use. The party needs to find who has been using it.
An underground spring has been drying up. The source is in a dungeon. Whatever is blocking it is doing so for reasons that will become apparent when the party gets there.

A magical catastrophe is imminent at the surface. The mechanism driving it is underground. It can be stopped, but the mechanism is guarded by something that was placed there specifically to stop intervention.

A dungeon's deepest room contains a being that has been imprisoned since before the current age of the world. It has communicated a willingness to be released in exchange for something. Someone is considering the exchange.
A tunnel collapses while the party is already inside. The only way forward is through the dungeon they were avoiding by using the tunnel.

The party's previous employer left equipment in a dungeon and is now in a position of needing it, can't retrieve it themselves, and is prepared to make the party's previous employment feel retroactively more generous.
A map to a dungeon has three 'here there be monsters' notations. Two of them are wrong. One is an understatement. The party has been hired because the person who knows which is which needs partners.

The dungeon contains the answer to a question the party has been asking since before the campaign started. Someone arranged for them to find it — not a patron, not an organization, but a thing in the dungeon that has been watching, waiting for them specifically, and that has something it needs done in return.
